title: Encode and Decode Strings
description:
Design an encoding scheme that packs a list of strings into a single string, and a matching decoder that recovers the exact original list from that packed string. The strings may be empty and may contain any characters, including ones you might otherwise use as a delimiter -- your scheme must handle that safely.

To demonstrate both halves at once, implement a function that encodes a list to one string and then immediately decodes that string back, returning the recovered list (which must exactly equal the input).

**Example**

```
codec(["hello", "world"])
# -> ["hello", "world"]

codec(["", "a:b", "4#three", ""])
# -> ["", "a:b", "4#three", ""]
```
hints: A plain delimiter character breaks if that character can appear inside a string -- you need a scheme immune to that.
Prefix each string with its length so the decoder always knows exactly how many characters to consume next, regardless of their content.
Use a fixed separator (like '#') strictly between the length prefix and the string data, e.g. '5#hello', and read the length first to know where the next string starts.

solutions.name: Length-prefixed encoding
solutions.explanation: Encode each string as its length, a '#' separator, and then the raw string data, concatenating these chunks for the whole list -- e.g. "hello" becomes "5#hello". Because the length is read before the string itself, the decoder always knows exactly how many characters belong to the current string, so any character (including '#' or digits) inside the string is handled safely. Decoding walks the encoded string, reading digits up to the next '#' to get the length, then slicing out exactly that many characters as the next string, repeating until the encoded string is exhausted.
solutions.complexity: Time O(total characters), Space O(total characters)
